hard.next_step: Jun 19, 2026: Touched base with Christopher to determine the state of this project and downselection to RFP. JRA 5/12: Awaiting CIMA team to finish reviewing RFI responses and shortlist vendors.
hard.ais_why: Christopher Tandu's intro framed the need as a holistic CLM to replace Excel reminders and SharePoint storage; the 13-minute call surfaced no Merlin, agentic, or GenAI language. RFI is ~98% CLM-focused, targeting ERP integration with Dynamics/SAP/Oracle; AI was never raised as a requirement.

north_star_verdict.headline: A $50k early-stage CLM evaluation that has gone effectively dark since the 24 Nov 2025 intro call: single-threaded to Christopher Tandu, an IT procurement specialist with no budget authority, with the RFI he planned for Dec 2025 still unconfirmed as issued and the buyer's shortlist decision still outstanding. The 19 Jun rep check-in drew no reply, and the close date has been pushed three times to 24 Sep.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 25 Nov 2025, qualified 9 Jan 2026, now ~223 days old and still in Qualified (stage actually regressed from Formal Evaluation to Qualified on 9 Jan 2026). 80 days to close (24 Sep 2026). Only one recorded two-way buyer exchange (the 24 Nov 2025 intro call); everything since is rep-side (12 May and 19 Jun Next Step notes, three close-date pushes). Buyer last genuinely engaged ~7 months ago, well beyond any normal cadence. Reaching 24 Sep would require RFI completion, shortlist, tailored demo, use-case validation, commercials, negotiation and contracting in under three months, against a realistic 12-15 month enterprise CLM motion for a 600-project-manager buyer. Current buyer-side pace is zero, so the date is indefensible.
evidence: Only one recorded buyer conversation: the 24 Nov 2025 intro call with Christopher Tandu; no buyer-side activity since

Single-threaded to Christopher Tandu, an IT procurement specialist who himself said he has no CLM and no budget authority; the board and sponsor he references are unnamed
No Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or power-level stakeholder identified or engaged; no Salesforce contact roles on the opportunity
Agiloft named in Salesforce (Competitors__c) but no buyer sentiment or competitive-position intelligence exists
Primary threat is inertia: buyer runs Excel plus SharePoint today and just absorbed an ERP change, making a no-decision the most likely outcome

metrics.narrative: The pain is articulated but never quantified. Christopher Tandu described a fragmented status quo on the 24 Nov 2025 call: he personally tracks contract renewals by hand, saying 'I put mail in an Excel sheet someday to see, okay Christopher, be careful, the renewal is minus 90 days,' while roughly 600 project managers handle legal documents with 'no standard regarding how the contract is supposed to be managed.' He named a concrete failure mode, 'some people just face those plus 20% increase because they didn't say no, we do not renew,' which points at auto-renewal leakage as the measurable cost. No dollar figure, contract volume, cycle-time, or savings target was put on it. The move is to convert that auto-renewal-penalty story into a quantified business case (annual value of missed renewals, hours lost across 600 PMs) so the project can be justified against doing nothing.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified or engaged. Christopher Tandu, the only contact, openly positioned himself as operational, 'I, as a procurement person, I do not have a CLM,' and pointed authority elsewhere: 'we might see internally with the board and the sponsor if we go on RFP,' and separately referenced 'my committee regarding the vision' (24 Nov 2025 call). Those figures are unnamed, and there are no Salesforce contact roles, so budget authority cannot be attributed to a real person. What the evidence does show is that the RFI-to-RFP decision, and therefore the budget, sits with a board/sponsor and a buying committee Christopher gatekeeps. The move is to ask Christopher to name and introduce that sponsor so the economic buyer can be mapped before any demo investment.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are visible from the intro call and the RFI framing even though no formal scorecard exists. Christopher Tandu made ERP integration a first-order test, asking directly 'in terms of integration with ERP, do you integrate yourself with Microsoft Dynamics,' after noting CIMA recently changed its ERP. He prioritised a holistic CLM 'ideally procurement and sales' that standardises contract handling across the organisation, and he valued Zycus doing its own implementation, confirming 'so editor and integrator as well, okay' when told there is no third-party handoff. The AIS note records the RFI is ~98% CLM-focused with ERP integration to Dynamics/SAP/Oracle as the core ask and AI absent. These are inferred-from-discovery rather than a confirmed weighted RFI scorecard; the move is to obtain the written RFI to lock the ranked criteria.

decision_process.narrative: Christopher Tandu described the process he intends to run but has not executed it. He said he will not release the RFI until the downstream path is set, 'I won't send that RFI if I do not know what will be the next step after that RFI,' and wants a demo tied to his questions delivered once, 'if we have a demo, it'll be with everyone involved in this project, Christopher and the IT team and the integrator.' The gating sequence he named is RFI, then internal review with the committee, then a board/sponsor decision on whether to go to RFP, then a scoped demo. The risk is that this process has stalled: the RFI he hoped to issue before Christmas 2025 is still unconfirmed as sent, and the 19 Jun 2026 status check went unanswered. The move is to re-confirm where the RFI review actually stands and secure the multi-party scoping call he described.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have been discussed. There is no reference to MSA, SOW, order form, legal review, procurement channel, or InfoSec on the only recorded call, and Current_Contract_Expiration__c is empty, so there is no incumbent-contract forcing function. The only adjacent signal is that CIMA just completed an ERP change and expects a 'massive cultural change,' implying IT and change-management approvals will matter at contracting for a 600-user rollout. Because the deal is still pre-RFP, paper process is genuinely unknown rather than a hygiene gap. The move is to surface, during the demo-scoping conversation, who owns legal, IT security, and procurement approval, so the path from selection to signature is visible before the date is committed.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is clearly confirmed and owned by Christopher Tandu. He characterised the current state as messy: no CLM, renewals tracked manually in Excel, contracts sitting 'somewhere in SharePoint on my desktop,' and roughly 600 project managers managing legal documents with no shared standard so 'some people track the end of the contract, some people do not' (24 Nov 2025 call). The financial edge of that pain is auto-renewal exposure, contracts silently rolling with a '+20% increase' because no one gave notice. There is also a change-management pain: CIMA 'just changed the ERP a few months ago,' so adoption fatigue is real. The confirmed pain owner is Christopher at the operational level; the executive pain owner who feels the leakage financially is not yet mapped. The move is to tie this pain to a number and to the board sponsor who bears the cost.

champion.narrative: There is no champion, only a cooperative gatekeeper. Christopher Tandu is engaged and transparent, he did his own homework, found Zycus via Gartner, and openly walked through CIMA's mess, but he is an IT procurement specialist with no CLM and no budget authority, and he controls access rather than opening it ('I'm still discussing with my committee regarding the vision'). He has not introduced the board, the sponsor, the IT team, or the integrator he references, and his engagement has cooled, the 19 Jun 2026 status check drew no reply. What is developed is rapport and a clear articulation of pain; what is missing is any evidence of internal advocacy, access to power, or him driving the agenda for Zycus. The move is to earn a multi-threaded scoping call that either elevates Christopher into a real champion or reaches the sponsor directly.

competition.narrative: Agiloft is the only named rival (Salesforce Competitors__c), but no buyer sentiment toward it exists on any call, so its shortlist status and the buyer's lean are unknown. On the evidence, the more immediate competitor is the status quo: CIMA runs Excel plus SharePoint with no CLM today and just absorbed an ERP change, so a no-decision or an indefinite delay is the likeliest way this deal is lost, not a head-to-head to Agiloft. Zycus's wedge from the intro call is a holistic CLM across procurement and sales with native Microsoft Dynamics integration and fully in-house implementation and integration (Christopher reacted positively to 'editor and integrator as well'). The move is to make that integrated, single-vendor delivery story the centrepiece of the scoped demo and to surface Agiloft's actual position when re-engaging the committee.

deal_movement.summary: The only genuine buyer movement is the 24 Nov 2025 intro call. Everything since is rep-side administration: the stage regressed to Qualified on 9 Jan 2026, the close date has been pushed three times, and two Next Step notes (12 May, 19 Jun) record the rep chasing an RFI review that has never confirmed forward.

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is inertia, not a vendor: CIMA manages contracts in Excel and SharePoint today and just absorbed an ERP change, so a no-decision or indefinite stall is the most probable loss path (24 Nov 2025 call). Agiloft is the only named rival (Salesforce Competitors__c) but carries no dated buyer sentiment, so its status is unknown rather than proven ahead. Zycus's live wedge is a holistic cross-org CLM with native Dynamics integration and fully in-house implementation.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: A $50k early-stage CLM evaluation gone dark since the 24 Nov 2025 intro call: single-threaded to Christopher Tandu, an IT procurement specialist with no budget authority, with the RFI still unconfirmed as issued and the biggest threat being CIMA simply doing nothing.

win_position.text: This deal stands weak: seven months since the only buyer call (24 Nov 2025), no economic buyer met, and the board sponsor Christopher named remains unintroduced.

Product fit is genuinely strong, Christopher wanted a holistic procurement-plus-sales CLM with native Dynamics integration and reacted well to Zycus doing its own implementation ('editor and integrator as well', 24 Nov 2025).
The most likely loss is a no-decision, not Agiloft, CIMA runs Excel and SharePoint today and just absorbed an ERP change, so inertia is winning.
The close date has been pushed three times to 24 Sep 2026 with zero buyer-side progression, so the forecast is indefensible.

deal_momentum.text: Momentum is negative: the stage regressed from Formal Evaluation to Qualified on 9 Jan 2026 and no buyer step has advanced since.
The 19 Jun 2026 rep check on downselect to RFP drew no buyer reply, confirming the RFI review has stalled.

customer_commitment.text: Christopher committed to nothing beyond eventually sending an RFI, and even that is gated on his committee ('I'm still discussing with my committee regarding the vision', 24 Nov 2025).
No demo, workshop, or next meeting has been scheduled by the buyer; the relationship is a single, cooling operational thread.

deal_risk.text: No economic buyer, decision maker, or champion is engaged, and there are no Salesforce contact roles on the opportunity.
Post-ERP change fatigue across 600 project managers makes a delayed or abandoned decision the base case unless a quantified value story forces action.
